text: How to {{c1::reconcile}} the fact that they are doing so well on evals? You look at the evals and you go, “Those are pretty hard evals.”
original: How to reconcile the fact that they are doing so well on evals? You look at the evals and you go, “Those are pretty hard evals.”
chunk_text:
# Ilya Sutskever — We're moving from the age of scaling to the age of research

Ilya Sutskever 00:01:38

Yeah. This is one of the very confusing things about the models right now. How to reconcile the fact that they are doing so well on evals? You look at the evals and you go, “Those are pretty hard evals.” They are doing so well. But the economic impact seems to be dramatically behind. It’s very difficult to make sense of, how can the model, on the one hand, do these amazing things, and then on the other hand, repeat itself twice in some situation?

text: They are doing so well. But the {{c1::economic impact}} seems to be {{c1::dramatically behind}}.

chunk_text:
# Ilya Sutskever — We're moving from the age of scaling to the age of research

Ilya Sutskever 00:01:38

Yeah. This is one of the very confusing things about the models right now. How to reconcile the fact that they are doing so well on evals? You look at the evals and you go, “Those are pretty hard evals.” They are doing so well. But the economic impact seems to be dramatically behind. It’s very difficult to make sense of, how can the model, on the one hand, do these amazing things, and then on the other hand, repeat itself twice in some situation?

text: Then you tell the model, “Can you please fix the bug?” And the model says, “Oh my God, you’re so right.” And it {{c1::introduces}} a second bug.
original: Then you tell the model, “Can you please fix the bug?” And the model says, “Oh my God, you’re so right.” And it introduces a second bug.
chunk_text: An example would be, let’s say you use vibe coding to do something. You go to some place and then you get a bug. Then you tell the model, “Can you please fix the bug?” And the model says, “Oh my God, you’re so right. I have a bug. Let me go fix that.” And it introduces a second bug. Then you tell it, “You have this new second bug,” and it tells you, “Oh my God, how could I have done it? You’re so right again,” and brings back the first bug, and you can alternate between those. How is that possible? I’m not sure, but it does suggest that something strange is going on.

text: Then you tell it, “You have this new second bug,” and it brings back the first bug, and you can {{c1::alternate between}} those.
original: Then you tell it, “You have this new second bug,” and it brings back the first bug, and you can alternate between those.

How is that possible? I’m not sure, but it does suggest that something strange is going on.

text: The more {{c1::whimsical}} explanation is that maybe RL training makes the models a little too {{c2::single-minded}} and narrowly focused.
original: The more whimsical explanation is that maybe RL training makes the models a little too single-minded and narrowly focused.
note_hint: whimsical: 异想天开的; single-minded: 一心一意的/死脑筋的
chunk_text: I have two possible explanations. The more whimsical explanation is that maybe RL training makes the models a little too single-minded and narrowly focused, a little bit too unaware, even though it also makes them aware in some other ways. Because of this, they can’t do basic things.

text: The models are a little bit too {{c1::unaware}}, {{c2::even though}} it also makes them aware in some other ways.
original: The models are a little bit too unaware, even though it also makes them aware in some other ways.
note_hint: unaware: 不觉察的; even though: 尽管
chunk_text: I have two possible explanations. The more whimsical explanation is that maybe RL training makes the models a little too single-minded and narrowly focused, a little bit too unaware, even though it also makes them aware in some other ways. Because of this, they can’t do basic things.

text: Back when people were doing {{c1::pre-training}}, the question of what data to train on was {{c2::answered}}, because that answer was everything.
original: Back when people were doing pre-training, the question of what data to train on was answered, because that answer was everything.
note_hint: pre-training: 预训练; answered: 得到解决/回答
chunk_text: But there is another explanation. Back when people were doing pre-training, the question of what data to train on was answered, because that answer was everything. When you do pre-training, you need all the data. So you don’t have to think if it’s going to be this data or that data.

text: When you do pre-training, you need all the data. So you {{c1::don’t have to think}} if it’s going to be this data or that data.
original: When you do pre-training, you need all the data. So you don’t have to think if it’s going to be this data or that data.
note_hint: don’t have to think: 不必思考/纠结
chunk_text: But there is another explanation. Back when people were doing pre-training, the question of what data to train on was answered, because that answer was everything. When you do pre-training, you need all the data. So you don’t have to think if it’s going to be this data or that data.
